attributes.headline: The Library as Public Living Room
attributes.body:
Walk into any thriving urban library on a weekday afternoon and you'll find: students at the tables, parents with strollers, retirees with newspapers, freelancers on laptops, a person reading a real book, someone using the bathroom they don't have to buy a coffee to access.

It's the most under-counted piece of urban infrastructure we have.

## What the library quietly does

- Heated and air-conditioned space, free, with no purchase required
- Bathrooms, drinking water, plug sockets
- A place to take a meeting if you don't have an office
- Public computers for the third of the city without good internet at home
- A space where being alone in public is normal — the disappearing third place
- Childcare during story hour
- The only legitimate place in the city center where a teenager can spend an afternoon without being chased away

## Why we keep cutting them

Because the return on investment is dispersed across a dozen public budgets (housing, healthcare, education, sanitation) and the cost shows up in one (the library's).

A branch library serving 50,000 people costs less per year than a single police precinct's overtime budget and probably prevents more actual harm.

## What works

The libraries that thrive in 2025 have a few things in common: long opening hours, fast Wi-Fi, comfortable chairs, and staff who treat being there as a right rather than a privilege.

They're modest interventions. They're also the closest thing modern cities have to a working commons.

featuredImage.caption: A public library reading room.
featuredImage.altText: Interior of a public library with tables, lamps, and rows of bookshelves.

cardInfo.summary: The most under-counted piece of urban infrastructure we have — and the closest thing modern cities have to a working commons.
